content.title: Malaysia: A Techno-Economic Analysis of Power Generation
content.summary: BloombergNEF techno-economic analysis of Malaysia's power generation. Solar PV is the cheapest new generation source at $33-61/MWh, below CCGT ($78-89/MWh) and coal ($56-77/MWh). Covers CAPEX benchmarks, cross-technology LCOE comparison, hydrogen/ammonia retrofitting costs, and long-term cost trajectory to 2050. Includes grid expansion cost implications of scaling solar.

content.title: Malaysia Energy Transition Outlook 2023
content.summary: IRENA's comprehensive Malaysia Energy Transition Outlook covering long-term pathway to 2050. Includes historical LSS auction bid price trends (2016-2020), electricity tariff structure before and after Incentive-Based Regulation, regional electricity price comparison for business sector, and grid integration challenges. Identifies solar PV as the key technology requiring up to 150GW by 2050. Co-published with Malaysia's Ministry of Natural Resources.

rag.warning: Published 2023 — some programme details (NEM, ATAP) may be outdated. Use SEDA and IEA-PVPS 2025 sources for current programme status.

content.title: Solar and Grid Flexibility Critical for Malaysia's Future Electricity Affordability and Security
content.summary: Ember's regional analysis of Malaysia's solar cost competitiveness and grid flexibility needs. Compares solar generation costs against coal and gas across Peninsular, Sabah, and Sarawak. Key finding: Peninsular can absorb 2.4GW more solar before storage is required; Sabah grid quota nearly exhausted at 150MW vs Peninsular 2,060MW. Reveals 30%+ revenue difference across regions due to tariff gap (28 vs 40 sen/kWh). Used for both Dimension 1 cost analysis and Dimension 2 grid capacity evidence.

rag.unknown_unknown_note: Sabah LSS quota only 150MW vs Peninsular 2,060MW — quota may already be near exhaustion. Regional tariff gap (28 vs 40 sen/kWh) means project revenue differs by 30%+ depending on region. Neither gap is visible in official policy documents — developers reading only SEDA guidelines would miss this entirely.

source.organization: IEA-PVPS (International Energy Agency Photovoltaic Power Systems Programme)

content.title: IEA-PVPS Malaysia Country Overview 2025
content.programme: FiT / NEM / LSS / SELCO / ATAP / CRESS / CREAM / CGPP / GTFS / SolaRIS
content.summary: Most comprehensive English-language overview of all Malaysia solar programmes as of 2025. Covers complete programme evolution from FiT (2011) through NEM 3.0 termination (June 2025) to Solar ATAP launch (January 2026). Documents CRESS (2024), CREAM, CGPP, GTFS 4.0 (RM1B until Dec 2025), and SolaRIS rebate. Explicitly notes Sarawak (SET-P) and Sabah (ECoS) operate under separate independent regulatory frameworks. Module prices remain low due to Chinese oversupply despite US tariffs.

rag.unknown_unknown_note: Sarawak operates under SET-P and Sabah under ECoS — both independent from federal SEDA programmes. Federal ATAP and NEM do not apply in Sarawak. GTFS 4.0 expires December 2025 — developers applying after this date lose RM1B green financing access. Module price risk from US anti-dumping duties on Malaysian panels partially offset by Chinese oversupply.

part1_cost_economics.filename: Solar Energy in Malaysia Market - Size, Share & Industry Analysis.html

content.title: Solar Energy in Malaysia Market — Size, Share & Industry Analysis
content.summary: Mordor Intelligence market overview of Malaysia's solar energy sector. Key data: LSS5 awarded 2GW in 2024 at average RM0.1699/kWh, with LSS PETRA 5+ targeting another 2GW in 2025. Market projected at 34.5% CAGR through 2030. Includes irradiance data (4.5-5.5 kWh/m²/day), capacity factor benchmarks (1.2-1.5 MWh/kWp/year), and competitive landscape with JinkoSolar and LONGi local assembly operations.

rag.warning: Partial content only — full report requires paid subscription. Key numbers (LSS5 tariff, capacity figures) available in free preview. Cross-reference with SEDA official sources for authoritative figures.

content.title: Solar Panel Installation Cost in Malaysia — Commercial CAPEX Benchmark
content.summary: Real-world CAPEX pricing from a registered Malaysian solar installer (800+ completed projects). Provides market rate benchmarks across system sizes: 500kWp at approximately RM2,100/kWp and 1MWp total at RM1.8M. Used to cross-validate government LCOE benchmarks against actual installer market pricing. Note: installer pricing reflects competitive market including labour, equipment, and margin — not pure equipment cost.

rag.warning: Industry source, not government official. Prices reflect a single installer's market rates and may vary. Use to cross-check BNEF/IRENA CAPEX figures against real-world market pricing, not as standalone authoritative source.
